problem_summary: 2023 MCM Problem C (data type): analyze a year of NYT Wordle daily report data (~360 days; columns: contest #, word, number of reported results, number in hard mode, and % distribution of guesses 1–6 + X). Five tasks: (1) explain/predict the number of reported results, give a prediction interval for the count on 2023-03-01, and assess how word attributes affect the hard-mode percentage; (2) build a model to predict the distribution of guess outcomes (1–6/X) for a given word/date, specifically EERIE, and quantify uncertainty; (3) classify words by difficulty and identify the driving attributes, classify EERIE, and report accuracy; (4) describe other interesting features of the dataset; (5) write a letter to the NYT Puzzle Editor.
models_used: Facebook Prophet additive time-series model (trend g(t) via piecewise-linear changepoints + Fourier-series weekly/yearly seasonality s(t) + holiday term h(t) + Gaussian noise) for forecasting the number of reported results, with an 80% prediction interval -> [10355, 18742] for 2023-03-01

Higher-order partial correlation analysis (recursive partial-correlation formula controlling for all other word attributes) to relate word attributes to the hard-mode percentage, after plain Pearson correlation failed

An 'optimized multi-objective regression prediction framework' = a model-selection harness that grid-searches and benchmarks six regressors (Partial Least Squares, Bayesian linear, Elastic Net, LASSO, Ridge, Quantile regression) by repeated-fit mean RMSE; LASSO (L1 penalty) chosen as best (RMSE 0.80) to predict the 7-bin guess distribution for EERIE -> (0,4,17,34,30,13,2)
Permutation Importance on the fitted LASSO model to rank attribute influence (top: non-repeat consonants 4.226, non-repeat vowels 3.993, letter-frequency sum 1.253)

K-means clustering (Euclidean distance, k chosen via Silhouette + Davies-Bouldin index; k=3) on the LASSO-predicted average number of tries to bin all dictionary words into easy/medium/hard (thresholds <4.13 / 4.13–4.37 / >=4.37)
Quartile/IQR (box-plot, k=0.5) method to convert clusters into per-attribute difficulty intervals, plus an interval-matching voting algorithm to validate classification (reported accuracy 91.36%)
key_techniques: Feature engineering of word attributes from raw strings: vowel/consonant counts, non-repeated (distinct) vowel/consonant counts, number of duplicate letters, max repeat count, word commonness rank, and summed per-letter corpus frequency — this attribute table is the backbone reused across all three tasks

Data cleaning of 'dirty' rows: reconstructing 4-letter entries (clen, tash) and misspellings (naïve->naive, marxh->marsh), stripping a stray space in 'favor'; 3-sigma (68-95-99.7) outlier rejection (zeroed the anomalous 'study' count); StandardScaler z-score normalization
Justifying model choice by dataset size: explicitly rejecting neural networks because only ~360 points exist, motivating small-data-friendly linear/regularized models and Prophet

Diagnosing and fixing a failed analysis: when Pearson r ~0.1 (insignificant) for attributes vs hard%, switching to higher-order partial correlation to control inter-attribute collinearity — flipped signs and produced significant moderate correlations (p << 0.05)

Two-stage difficulty pipeline: use the Task-2 regression (with time variable removed) to impute average tries for all 12,974 dictionary words, then cluster those imputed averages — bootstrapping labels the data didn't directly contain
Multiple-run averaging for stability (averaging permutation-importance over 3 runs; averaging repeated LASSO fits for the EERIE prediction)
Cluster-count selection via two complementary internal metrics (Silhouette + DBI) rather than a single elbow
why_it_won: "It addressed every sub-task end to end with a defensible, dataset-appropriate method and connected the tasks into one coherent pipeline (shared attribute table -> Prophet/partial-correlation -> LASSO distribution -> K-means difficulty -> EERIE verdict). It showed modeling maturity judges reward: explicitly matching method to the small-data regime (rejecting NNs), and — most distinctively — diagnosing a failed Pearson analysis and recovering with higher-order partial correlation that controls for attribute collinearity. The 'multi-objective regression framework' frames model selection as a principled grid-search benchmark across six regressors with RMSE evidence rather than an arbitrary pick, and every claim carries a number (RMSE 0.80, accuracy 91.36%, importance weights with ± std, correlation coefficients with p-values). It also delivered the required deliverables cleanly: a concrete prediction interval [10355,18742], the EERIE distribution (0,4,17,34,30,13,2) and 'difficult' verdict cross-checked two independent ways (regression average tries 4.38 AND attribute-interval matching), a genuine sensitivity analysis varying the test-set fraction 0.1–0.3, and a polished NYT letter with actionable product suggestions."
section_highlights: "Strong structure: standard MCM scaffold (Summary -> Assumptions+Notations -> Data Processing -> Task1/2/3 -> Interesting aspects -> Sensitivity -> Strengths/Weaknesses -> Letter) with a one-page model-framework flowchart up front mapping data->methods->outputs. The Summary front-loads every quantitative result. Each task subsection follows a repeatable rhythm: algorithm background/motivation -> principle with numbered equations -> parameter settings -> results table -> reliability/validation. Reliability/uncertainty is treated as a first-class subsection per task (RMSE checks, permutation importance, interval-matching validation), not an afterthought. The 'EERIE' worked example threads through Tasks 2 and 3 as a concrete anchor. The cross-validation of the EERIE verdict by two independent routes and the explicit Pearson-fails-then-partial-correlation narrative are standout judge-pleasers. The letter restates findings in plain language with two concrete recommendations (multiplayer challenge mode, kids mode)."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The headline forecast of ~10k–19k reports for 2023-03-01 is essentially naive trend extrapolation of a sharply declining curve; Prophet on a near-monotonic decay with one seasonal cycle of training data is fragile and the interval may be optimistic

Conceptual mismatch in Task 2: the target is a per-word guess distribution that depends on word attributes, yet they include 'Date' as a feature and frame it as forecasting 'a future date' — for a fixed word EERIE the date dependence is weakly justified, and predicting a probability distribution with plain LASSO (independent per-bin regression) ignores the simplex constraint (bins should sum to 100 and be non-negative; they got there by rounding)

The 'multi-objective regression prediction framework' is essentially a model-selection grid search over standard sklearn regressors dressed up with grand terminology; it is not genuinely multi-objective and the cited multi-objective-regression references are a loose fit

Difficulty labels are bootstrapped from the model's own predicted average tries, then the same model validates them — partly circular; the 91.36% 'accuracy' comes from an idiosyncratic interval-matching voting scheme, not a held-out classification benchmark, so it overstates rigor

Higher-order partial correlation flips signs versus Pearson, which they present as a fix but could equally signal multicollinearity instability; results are reported without confidence intervals on the partial coefficients

Some numbers are internally loose/contradictory (e.g., narrative says vowels+non-repeats+commonness negatively correlated while a table lists commonness as 'no correlation'; importance text vs the ±std table name slightly different top features), and AI/'artificial intelligence algorithms' is hand-waved for reconstructing dirty words
Data leakage risk: StandardScaler and feature selection appear applied before/around the train-test split rather than fit on train only
reusable_patterns: Build one canonical engineered-feature table from the raw entities early in Data Processing, then reuse it as the shared input to every downstream task — gives the paper coherence and saves re-derivation
For small datasets, explicitly justify rejecting deep learning and default to regularized linear models (LASSO/Ridge/Elastic Net) + Prophet; state the data-size reason in writing

Frame model selection as a benchmark harness: grid-search hyperparameters across a panel of candidate regressors, rank by repeated-fit mean RMSE, and report the table — turns an arbitrary choice into evidence

When simple correlation is insignificant due to collinear predictors, escalate to partial/higher-order partial correlation controlling for the other variables, and narrate the before/after to show diagnostic skill

Two-stage label bootstrapping: use a regression model to impute a continuous difficulty proxy (average tries) for the full dictionary, then cluster that proxy with K-means to create difficulty classes the raw data lacked
Select k with two internal indices (Silhouette + Davies-Bouldin) and tabulate them rather than relying on a single elbow plot
Use Permutation Importance (model-agnostic, averaged over several runs with ±std) to produce a defensible feature-influence ranking for any fitted model
Thread a single concrete worked example (here EERIE) through multiple tasks and cross-verify the final verdict via two independent methods
Treat per-task reliability/validation and a parameter-sweep sensitivity analysis (vary test-set fraction, report % change in RMSE) as mandatory standalone sections
Open with a model-framework flowchart and a results-laden summary; close with a plain-language stakeholder letter containing concrete, actionable recommendations
